name: httpx
documentation:
HTTPX - A next-generation HTTP client for Python.

HTTPX is a fully featured HTTP client library for Python 3. It includes **an integrated command line client**, has support for both **HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2**, and provides both **sync and async APIs**. --- Install HTTPX using pip: ```shell pip install httpx ``` Now, let's get started: ```pycon >>> import httpx >>> r = httpx.get('https://www.example.org/') >>> r >>> r.status_code 200 >>> r.headers['content-type'] 'text/html; charset=UTF-8' >>> r.text '\n\n\nExample Domain...' ``` Or, using the command-line client. ```shell pip install 'httpx[cli]' # The command line client is an optional dependency. ``` Which now allows us to use HTTPX directly from the command-line...

Sending a request...

## Features HTTPX builds on the well-established usability of `requests`, and gives you: * A broadly [requests-compatible API](https://www.python-httpx.org/compatibility/). * An integrated command-line client. * HTTP/1.1 [and HTTP/2 support](https://www.python-httpx.org/http2/). * Standard synchronous interface, but with [async support if you need it](https://www.python-httpx.org/async/). * Ability to make requests directly to [WSGI applications](https://www.python-httpx.org/advanced/transports/#wsgi-transport) or [ASGI applications](https://www.python-httpx.org/advanced/transports/#asgi-transport). * Strict timeouts everywhere. * Fully type annotated. * 100% test coverage. Plus all the standard features of `requests`... * International Domains and URLs * Keep-Alive & Connection Pooling * Sessions with Cookie Persistence * Browser-style SSL Verification * Basic/Digest Authentication * Elegant Key/Value Cookies * Automatic Decompression * Automatic Content Decoding * Unicode Response Bodies * Multipart File Uploads * HTTP(S) Proxy Support * Connection Timeouts * Streaming Downloads * .netrc Support * Chunked Requests ## Installation Install with pip: ```shell pip install httpx ``` Or, to include the optional HTTP/2 support, use: ```shell pip install httpx[http2] ``` HTTPX requires Python 3.8+. ## Documentation Project documentation is available at [https://www.python-httpx.org/](https://www.python-httpx.org/). For a run-through of all the basics, head over to the [QuickStart](https://www.python-httpx.org/quickstart/). For more advanced topics, see the [Advanced Usage](https://www.python-httpx.org/advanced/) section, the [async support](https://www.python-httpx.org/async/) section, or the [HTTP/2](https://www.python-httpx.org/http2/) section. The [Developer Interface](https://www.python-httpx.org/api/) provides a comprehensive API reference. To find out about tools that integrate with HTTPX, see [Third Party Packages](https://www.python-httpx.org/third_party_packages/). ## Contribute If you want to contribute with HTTPX check out the [Contributing Guide](https://www.python-httpx.org/contributing/) to learn how to start. ## Dependencies The HTTPX project relies on these excellent libraries: * `httpcore` - The underlying transport implementation for `httpx`. * `h11` - HTTP/1.1 support. * `certifi` - SSL certificates. * `idna` - Internationalized domain name support. * `sniffio` - Async library autodetection. As well as these optional installs: * `h2` - HTTP/2 support. *(Optional, with `httpx[http2]`)* * `socksio` - SOCKS proxy support. *(Optional, with `httpx[socks]`)* * `rich` - Rich terminal support. *(Optional, with `httpx[cli]`)* * `click` - Command line client support. *(Optional, with `httpx[cli]`)* * `brotli` or `brotlicffi` - Decoding for "brotli" compressed responses. *(Optional, with `httpx[brotli]`)* * `zstandard` - Decoding for "zstd" compressed responses. *(Optional, with `httpx[zstd]`)* A huge amount of credit is due to `requests` for the API layout that much of this work follows, as well as to `urllib3` for plenty of design inspiration around the lower-level networking details. ---

HTTPX is BSD licensed code.
Designed & crafted with care.
— 🦋 —

## Release Information ### Fixed * Reintroduced supposedly-private `URLTypes` shortcut. (#2673) --- [Full changelog](https://github.com/encode/httpx/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md)

modules.name: httpx._compat
documentation:
The _compat module is used for code which requires branching between different
Python environments. It is excluded from the code coverage checks.

methods.name: httpx._transports.base.BaseTransport.handle_request
documentation:
Send a single HTTP request and return a response.

Developers shouldn't typically ever need to call into this API directly,
since the Client class provides all the higher level user-facing API
niceties.

In order to properly release any network resources, the response
stream should *either* be consumed immediately, with a call to
`response.stream.read()`, or else the `handle_request` call should
be followed with a try/finally block to ensuring the stream is
always closed.

Example usage:

    with httpx.HTTPTransport() as transport:
        req = httpx.Request(
            method=b"GET",
            url=(b"https", b"www.example.com", 443, b"/"),
            headers=[(b"Host", b"www.example.com")],
        )
        resp = transport.handle_request(req)
        body = resp.stream.read()
        print(resp.status_code, resp.headers, body)


Takes a `Request` instance as the only argument.

Returns a `Response` instance.

classes.name: httpx._status_codes.codes

methods.name: httpx._status_codes.codes.is_informational
documentation: Returns `True` for 1xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._status_codes.codes.is_success
documentation: Returns `True` for 2xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._status_codes.codes.is_redirect
documentation: Returns `True` for 3xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._status_codes.codes.is_client_error
documentation: Returns `True` for 4xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._status_codes.codes.is_server_error
documentation: Returns `True` for 5xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._status_codes.codes.is_error
documentation: Returns `True` for 4xx or 5xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

HTTP status codes and reason phrases

Status codes from the following RFCs are all observed:

    * RFC 7231: Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP/1.1), obsoletes 2616
    * RFC 6585: Additional HTTP Status Codes
    * RFC 3229: Delta encoding in HTTP
    * RFC 4918: HTTP Extensions for WebDAV, obsoletes 2518
    * RFC 5842: Binding Extensions to WebDAV
    * RFC 7238: Permanent Redirect
    * RFC 2295: Transparent Content Negotiation in HTTP
    * RFC 2774: An HTTP Extension Framework
    * RFC 7540: Hypertext Transfer Protocol Version 2 (HTTP/2)
    * RFC 2324: Hyper Text Coffee Pot Control Protocol (HTCPCP/1.0)
    * RFC 7725: An HTTP Status Code to Report Legal Obstacles
    * RFC 8297: An HTTP Status Code for Indicating Hints
    * RFC 8470: Using Early Data in HTTP

classes.name: httpx._transports.wsgi.WSGITransport
documentation:
A custom transport that handles sending requests directly to an WSGI app.
The simplest way to use this functionality is to use the `app` argument.

```
client = httpx.Client(app=app)
```

Alternatively, you can setup the transport instance explicitly.
This allows you to include any additional configuration arguments specific
to the WSGITransport class:

```
transport = httpx.WSGITransport(
    app=app,
    script_name="/submount",
    remote_addr="1.2.3.4"
)
client = httpx.Client(transport=transport)
```

Arguments:

* `app` - The WSGI application.
* `raise_app_exceptions` - Boolean indicating if exceptions in the application
   should be raised. Default to `True`. Can be set to `False` for use cases
   such as testing the content of a client 500 response.
* `script_name` - The root path on which the WSGI application should be mounted.
* `remote_addr` - A string indicating the client IP of incoming requests.
```

classes.name: httpx._transports.asgi.ASGITransport
documentation:
A custom AsyncTransport that handles sending requests directly to an ASGI app.

```python
transport = httpx.ASGITransport(
    app=app,
    root_path="/submount",
    client=("1.2.3.4", 123)
)
client = httpx.AsyncClient(transport=transport)
```

Arguments:

* `app` - The ASGI application.
* `raise_app_exceptions` - Boolean indicating if exceptions in the application
   should be raised. Default to `True`. Can be set to `False` for use cases
   such as testing the content of a client 500 response.
* `root_path` - The root path on which the ASGI application should be mounted.
* `client` - A two-tuple indicating the client IP and port of incoming requests.
```

modules.name: httpx._types
documentation: Type definitions for type checking purposes.

methods.name: httpx._types.SyncByteStream.close
documentation:
Subclasses can override this method to release any network resources
after a request/response cycle is complete.

functions.name: httpx._content.encode_request
documentation:
Handles encoding the given `content`, `data`, `files`, and `json`,
returning a two-tuple of (<headers>, <stream>).

functions.name: httpx._content.encode_response
documentation:
Handles encoding the given `content`, returning a two-tuple of
(<headers>, <stream>).

classes.name: httpx._content.UnattachedStream
documentation:
If a request or response is serialized using pickle, then it is no longer
attached to a stream for I/O purposes. Any stream operations should result
in `httpx.StreamClosed`.

classes.name: httpx._multipart.DataField
documentation: A single form field item, within a multipart form field.

classes.name: httpx._multipart.FileField
documentation: A single file field item, within a multipart form field.

classes.name: httpx._multipart.MultipartStream

methods.name: httpx._multipart.MultipartStream.get_content_length
documentation:
Return the length of the multipart encoded content, or `None` if
any of the files have a length that cannot be determined upfront.

methods.name: httpx._multipart.MultipartStream.get_headers
documentation: Content stream interface.
Request content as streaming multipart encoded form data.

modules.name: httpx._exceptions
documentation:
Our exception hierarchy:

* HTTPError
  x RequestError
    + TransportError
      - TimeoutException
        · ConnectTimeout
        · ReadTimeout
        · WriteTimeout
        · PoolTimeout
      - NetworkError
        · ConnectError
        · ReadError
        · WriteError
        · CloseError
      - ProtocolError
        · LocalProtocolError
        · RemoteProtocolError
      - ProxyError
      - UnsupportedProtocol
    + DecodingError
    + TooManyRedirects
  x HTTPStatusError
* InvalidURL
* CookieConflict
* StreamError
  x StreamConsumed
  x StreamClosed
  x ResponseNotRead
  x RequestNotRead

functions.name: httpx._exceptions.request_context
documentation:
A context manager that can be used to attach the given request context
to any `RequestError` exceptions that are raised within the block.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.HTTPError
documentation:
Base class for `RequestError` and `HTTPStatusError`.

Useful for `try...except` blocks when issuing a request,
and then calling `.raise_for_status()`.

For example:

```
try:
    response = httpx.get("https://www.example.com")
    response.raise_for_status()
except httpx.HTTPError as exc:
    print(f"HTTP Exception for {exc.request.url} - {exc}")
```

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.RequestError
documentation: Base class for all exceptions that may occur when issuing a `.request()`.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.TransportError
documentation: Base class for all exceptions that occur at the level of the Transport API.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.TimeoutException
documentation:
The base class for timeout errors.

An operation has timed out.
Timeout exceptions...

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ConnectTimeout
documentation: Timed out while connecting to the host.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ReadTimeout
documentation: Timed out while receiving data from the host.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.WriteTimeout
documentation: Timed out while sending data to the host.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.PoolTimeout
documentation: Timed out waiting to acquire a connection from the pool.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.NetworkError
documentation:
The base class for network-related errors.

An error occurred while interacting with the network.
Core networking exceptions...

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ReadError
documentation: Failed to receive data from the network.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.WriteError
documentation: Failed to send data through the network.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ConnectError
documentation: Failed to establish a connection.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.CloseError
documentation: Failed to close a connection.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ProxyError
documentation: An error occurred while establishing a proxy connection.
Other transport exceptions...

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.UnsupportedProtocol
documentation:
Attempted to make a request to an unsupported protocol.

For example issuing a request to `ftp://www.example.com`.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ProtocolError
documentation: The protocol was violated.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.LocalProtocolError
documentation:
A protocol was violated by the client.

For example if the user instantiated a `Request` instance explicitly,
failed to include the mandatory `Host:` header, and then issued it directly
using `client.send()`.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.RemoteProtocolError
documentation:
The protocol was violated by the server.

For example, returning malformed HTTP.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.DecodingError
documentation: Decoding of the response failed, due to a malformed encoding.
Other request exceptions...

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.TooManyRedirects
documentation: Too many redirects.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.HTTPStatusError
documentation:
The response had an error HTTP status of 4xx or 5xx.

May be raised when calling `response.raise_for_status()`
Client errors

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.InvalidURL
documentation: URL is improperly formed or cannot be parsed.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.CookieConflict
documentation:
Attempted to lookup a cookie by name, but multiple cookies existed.

Can occur when calling `response.cookies.get(...)`.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.StreamError
documentation:
The base class for stream exceptions.

The developer made an error in accessing the request stream in
an invalid way.
Stream exceptions...
These may occur as the result of a programming error, by accessing
the request/response stream in an invalid manner.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.StreamConsumed
documentation:
Attempted to read or stream content, but the content has already
been streamed.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.StreamClosed
documentation:
Attempted to read or stream response content, but the request has been
closed.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.ResponseNotRead
documentation: Attempted to access streaming response content, without having called `read()`.

classes.name: httpx._exceptions.RequestNotRead
documentation: Attempted to access streaming request content, without having called `read()`.

classes.name: httpx._auth.Auth

methods.name: httpx._auth.Auth.auth_flow
documentation:
Execute the authentication flow.

To dispatch a request, `yield` it:

```
yield request
```

The client will `.send()` the response back into the flow generator. You can
access it like so:

```
response = yield request
```

A `return` (or reaching the end of the generator) will result in the
client returning the last response obtained from the server.

You can dispatch as many requests as is necessary.

methods.name: httpx._auth.Auth.sync_auth_flow
documentation:
Execute the authentication flow synchronously.

By default, this defers to `.auth_flow()`. You should override this method
when the authentication scheme does I/O and/or uses concurrency primitives.

methods.name: httpx._auth.Auth.async_auth_flow
documentation:
Execute the authentication flow asynchronously.

By default, this defers to `.auth_flow()`. You should override this method
when the authentication scheme does I/O and/or uses concurrency primitives.

Base class for all authentication schemes.

To implement a custom authentication scheme, subclass `Auth` and override
the `.auth_flow()` method.

If the authentication scheme does I/O such as disk access or network calls, or uses
synchronization primitives such as locks, you should override `.sync_auth_flow()`
and/or `.async_auth_flow()` instead of `.auth_flow()` to provide specialized
implementations that will be used by `Client` and `AsyncClient` respectively.

classes.name: httpx._auth.FunctionAuth
documentation:
Allows the 'auth' argument to be passed as a simple callable function,
that takes the request, and returns a new, modified request.

classes.name: httpx._auth.BasicAuth
documentation:
Allows the 'auth' argument to be passed as a (username, password) pair,
and uses HTTP Basic authentication.

classes.name: httpx._auth.NetRCAuth
documentation: Use a 'netrc' file to lookup basic auth credentials based on the url host.

methods.name: httpx._auth.DigestAuth._parse_challenge
documentation:
Returns a challenge from a Digest WWW-Authenticate header.
These take the form of:
`Digest realm="realm@host.com",qop="auth,auth-int",nonce="abc",opaque="xyz"`

classes.name: httpx._config.SSLConfig

methods.name: httpx._config.SSLConfig.load_ssl_context_no_verify
documentation: Return an SSL context for unverified connections.

methods.name: httpx._config.SSLConfig.load_ssl_context_verify
documentation: Return an SSL context for verified connections.

methods.name: httpx._config.SSLConfig._create_default_ssl_context
documentation:
Creates the default SSLContext object that's used for both verified
and unverified connections.

methods.name: httpx._config.SSLConfig._load_client_certs
documentation: Loads client certificates into our SSLContext object
SSL Configuration.

classes.name: httpx._config.Timeout
documentation:
Timeout configuration.

**Usage**:

Timeout(None)               # No timeouts.
Timeout(5.0)                # 5s timeout on all operations.
Timeout(None, connect=5.0)  # 5s timeout on connect, no other timeouts.
Timeout(5.0, connect=10.0)  # 10s timeout on connect. 5s timeout elsewhere.
Timeout(5.0, pool=None)     # No timeout on acquiring connection from pool.
                            # 5s timeout elsewhere.

classes.name: httpx._config.Limits
documentation:
Configuration for limits to various client behaviors.

**Parameters:**

* **max_connections** - The maximum number of concurrent connections that may be
        established.
* **max_keepalive_connections** - Allow the connection pool to maintain
        keep-alive connections below this point. Should be less than or equal
        to `max_connections`.
* **keepalive_expiry** - Time limit on idle keep-alive connections in seconds.

modules.name: httpx._decoders
documentation:
Handlers for Content-Encoding.

See: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Encoding

classes.name: httpx._decoders.IdentityDecoder
documentation: Handle unencoded data.

classes.name: httpx._decoders.DeflateDecoder
documentation:
Handle 'deflate' decoding.

See: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1838699

classes.name: httpx._decoders.GZipDecoder
documentation:
Handle 'gzip' decoding.

See: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1838699

classes.name: httpx._decoders.BrotliDecoder
documentation:
Handle 'brotli' decoding.

Requires `pip install brotlipy`. See: https://brotlipy.readthedocs.io/
    or   `pip install brotli`. See https://github.com/google/brotli
Supports both 'brotlipy' and 'Brotli' packages since they share an import
name. The top branches are for 'brotlipy' and bottom branches for 'Brotli'

classes.name: httpx._decoders.ZStandardDecoder

methods.name: httpx._decoders.ZStandardDecoder.__init__
documentation: inspired by the ZstdDecoder implementation in urllib3

Handle 'zstd' RFC 8878 decoding.

Requires `pip install zstandard`.
Can be installed as a dependency of httpx using `pip install httpx[zstd]`.

classes.name: httpx._decoders.MultiDecoder

methods.name: httpx._decoders.MultiDecoder.__init__
documentation:
'children' should be a sequence of decoders in the order in which
each was applied.
Handle the case where multiple encodings have been applied.

classes.name: httpx._decoders.ByteChunker
documentation: Handles returning byte content in fixed-size chunks.

classes.name: httpx._decoders.TextChunker
documentation: Handles returning text content in fixed-size chunks.

classes.name: httpx._decoders.TextDecoder
documentation: Handles incrementally decoding bytes into text

classes.name: httpx._decoders.LineDecoder
documentation:
Handles incrementally reading lines from text.

Has the same behaviour as the stdllib splitlines,
but handling the input iteratively.

modules.name: httpx._transports.default
documentation:
Custom transports, with nicely configured defaults.

The following additional keyword arguments are currently supported by httpcore...

* uds: str
* local_address: str
* retries: int

Example usages...

# Disable HTTP/2 on a single specific domain.
mounts = {
    "all://": httpx.HTTPTransport(http2=True),
    "all://*example.org": httpx.HTTPTransport()
}

# Using advanced httpcore configuration, with connection retries.
transport = httpx.HTTPTransport(retries=1)
client = httpx.Client(transport=transport)

# Using advanced httpcore configuration, with unix domain sockets.
transport = httpx.HTTPTransport(uds="socket.uds")
client = httpx.Client(transport=transport)

modules.name: httpx._urlparse
documentation:
An implementation of `urlparse` that provides URL validation and normalization
as described by RFC3986.

We rely on this implementation rather than the one in Python's stdlib, because:

* It provides more complete URL validation.
* It properly differentiates between an empty querystring and an absent querystring,
  to distinguish URLs with a trailing '?'.
* It handles scheme, hostname, port, and path normalization.
* It supports IDNA hostnames, normalizing them to their encoded form.
* The API supports passing individual components, as well as the complete URL string.

Previously we relied on the excellent `rfc3986` package to handle URL parsing and
validation, but this module provides a simpler alternative, with less indirection
required.

functions.name: httpx._urlparse.validate_path
documentation:
Path validation rules that depend on if the URL contains
a scheme or authority component.

See https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3986.html#section-3.3

functions.name: httpx._urlparse.normalize_path
documentation:
Drop "." and ".." segments from a URL path.

For example:

    normalize_path("/path/./to/somewhere/..") == "/path/to"

functions.name: httpx._urlparse.percent_encoded
documentation: Use percent-encoding to quote a string.

functions.name: httpx._urlparse.quote
documentation:
Use percent-encoding to quote a string, omitting existing '%xx' escape sequences.

See: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.1

* `string`: The string to be percent-escaped.
* `safe`: A string containing characters that may be treated as safe, and do not
    need to be escaped. Unreserved characters are always treated as safe.
    See: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.3

functions.name: httpx._urlparse.urlencode
documentation:
We can use a much simpler version of the stdlib urlencode here because
we don't need to handle a bunch of different typing cases, such as bytes vs str.

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b2f7b2ef0b5421e01efb8c7bee2ef95d3bab77eb/Lib/urllib/parse.py#L926

Note that we use '%20' encoding for spaces. and '%2F  for '/'.
This is slightly different than `requests`, but is the behaviour that browsers use.

See
- https://github.com/encode/httpx/issues/2536
- https://github.com/encode/httpx/issues/2721
- https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.parse.html#urllib.parse.urlencode

classes.name: httpx._urls.URL

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.scheme
documentation:
The URL scheme, such as "http", "https".
Always normalised to lowercase.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.raw_scheme
documentation:
The raw bytes representation of the URL scheme, such as b"http", b"https".
Always normalised to lowercase.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.userinfo
documentation:
The URL userinfo as a raw bytestring.
For example: b"jo%40email.com:a%20secret".

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.username
documentation:
The URL username as a string, with URL decoding applied.
For example: "jo@email.com"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.password
documentation:
The URL password as a string, with URL decoding applied.
For example: "a secret"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.host
documentation:
The URL host as a string.
Always normalized to lowercase, with IDNA hosts decoded into unicode.

Examples:

url = httpx.URL("http://www.EXAMPLE.org")
assert url.host == "www.example.org"

url = httpx.URL("http://中国.icom.museum")
assert url.host == "中国.icom.museum"

url = httpx.URL("http://xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum")
assert url.host == "中国.icom.museum"

url = httpx.URL("https://[::ffff:192.168.0.1]")
assert url.host == "::ffff:192.168.0.1"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.raw_host
documentation:
The raw bytes representation of the URL host.
Always normalized to lowercase, and IDNA encoded.

Examples:

url = httpx.URL("http://www.EXAMPLE.org")
assert url.raw_host == b"www.example.org"

url = httpx.URL("http://中国.icom.museum")
assert url.raw_host == b"xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum"

url = httpx.URL("http://xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum")
assert url.raw_host == b"xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum"

url = httpx.URL("https://[::ffff:192.168.0.1]")
assert url.raw_host == b"::ffff:192.168.0.1"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.port
documentation:
The URL port as an integer.

Note that the URL class performs port normalization as per the WHATWG spec.
Default ports for "http", "https", "ws", "wss", and "ftp" schemes are always
treated as `None`.

For example:

assert httpx.URL("http://www.example.com") == httpx.URL("http://www.example.com:80")
assert httpx.URL("http://www.example.com:80").port is None

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.netloc
documentation:
Either `<host>` or `<host>:<port>` as bytes.
Always normalized to lowercase, and IDNA encoded.

This property may be used for generating the value of a request
"Host" header.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.path
documentation:
The URL path as a string. Excluding the query string, and URL decoded.

For example:

url = httpx.URL("https://example.com/pa%20th")
assert url.path == "/pa th"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.query
documentation:
The URL query string, as raw bytes, excluding the leading b"?".

This is necessarily a bytewise interface, because we cannot
perform URL decoding of this representation until we've parsed
the keys and values into a QueryParams instance.

For example:

url = httpx.URL("https://example.com/?filter=some%20search%20terms")
assert url.query == b"filter=some%20search%20terms"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.params
documentation:
The URL query parameters, neatly parsed and packaged into an immutable
multidict representation.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.raw_path
documentation:
The complete URL path and query string as raw bytes.
Used as the target when constructing HTTP requests.

For example:

GET /users?search=some%20text HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.org
Connection: close

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.fragment
documentation:
The URL fragments, as used in HTML anchors.
As a string, without the leading '#'.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.raw
documentation:
Provides the (scheme, host, port, target) for the outgoing request.

In older versions of `httpx` this was used in the low-level transport API.
We no longer use `RawURL`, and this property will be deprecated
in a future release.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.is_absolute_url
documentation:
Return `True` for absolute URLs such as 'http://example.com/path',
and `False` for relative URLs such as '/path'.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.is_relative_url
documentation:
Return `False` for absolute URLs such as 'http://example.com/path',
and `True` for relative URLs such as '/path'.

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.copy_with
documentation:
Copy this URL, returning a new URL with some components altered.
Accepts the same set of parameters as the components that are made
available via properties on the `URL` class.

For example:

url = httpx.URL("https://www.example.com").copy_with(
    username="jo@gmail.com", password="a secret"
)
assert url == "https://jo%40email.com:a%20secret@www.example.com"

methods.name: httpx._urls.URL.join
documentation:
Return an absolute URL, using this URL as the base.

Eg.

url = httpx.URL("https://www.example.com/test")
url = url.join("/new/path")
assert url == "https://www.example.com/new/path"

url = httpx.URL("HTTPS://jo%40email.com:a%20secret@müller.de:1234/pa%20th?search=ab#anchorlink")

assert url.scheme == "https"
assert url.username == "jo@email.com"
assert url.password == "a secret"
assert url.userinfo == b"jo%40email.com:a%20secret"
assert url.host == "müller.de"
assert url.raw_host == b"xn--mller-kva.de"
assert url.port == 1234
assert url.netloc == b"xn--mller-kva.de:1234"
assert url.path == "/pa th"
assert url.query == b"?search=ab"
assert url.raw_path == b"/pa%20th?search=ab"
assert url.fragment == "anchorlink"

The components of a URL are broken down like this:

   https://jo%40email.com:a%20secret@müller.de:1234/pa%20th?search=ab#anchorlink
[scheme]   [  username  ] [password] [ host ][port][ path ] [ query ] [fragment]
           [       userinfo        ] [   netloc   ][    raw_path    ]

Note that:

* `url.scheme` is normalized to always be lowercased.

* `url.host` is normalized to always be lowercased. Internationalized domain
  names are represented in unicode, without IDNA encoding applied. For instance:

  url = httpx.URL("http://中国.icom.museum")
  assert url.host == "中国.icom.museum"
  url = httpx.URL("http://xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum")
  assert url.host == "中国.icom.museum"

* `url.raw_host` is normalized to always be lowercased, and is IDNA encoded.

  url = httpx.URL("http://中国.icom.museum")
  assert url.raw_host == b"xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum"
  url = httpx.URL("http://xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum")
  assert url.raw_host == b"xn--fiqs8s.icom.museum"

* `url.port` is either None or an integer. URLs that include the default port for
  "http", "https", "ws", "wss", and "ftp" schemes have their port
  normalized to `None`.

  assert httpx.URL("http://example.com") == httpx.URL("http://example.com:80")
  assert httpx.URL("http://example.com").port is None
  assert httpx.URL("http://example.com:80").port is None

* `url.userinfo` is raw bytes, without URL escaping. Usually you'll want to work
  with `url.username` and `url.password` instead, which handle the URL escaping.

* `url.raw_path` is raw bytes of both the path and query, without URL escaping.
  This portion is used as the target when constructing HTTP requests. Usually you'll
  want to work with `url.path` instead.

* `url.query` is raw bytes, without URL escaping. A URL query string portion can
  only be properly URL escaped when decoding the parameter names and values
  themselves.

classes.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.keys
documentation:
Return all the keys in the query params.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456&b=789")
assert list(q.keys()) == ["a", "b"]

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.values
documentation:
Return all the values in the query params. If a key occurs more than once
only the first item for that key is returned.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456&b=789")
assert list(q.values()) == ["123", "789"]

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.items
documentation:
Return all items in the query params. If a key occurs more than once
only the first item for that key is returned.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456&b=789")
assert list(q.items()) == [("a", "123"), ("b", "789")]

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.multi_items
documentation:
Return all items in the query params. Allow duplicate keys to occur.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456&b=789")
assert list(q.multi_items()) == [("a", "123"), ("a", "456"), ("b", "789")]

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.get
documentation:
Get a value from the query param for a given key. If the key occurs
more than once, then only the first value is returned.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456&b=789")
assert q.get("a") == "123"

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.get_list
documentation:
Get all values from the query param for a given key.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456&b=789")
assert q.get_list("a") == ["123", "456"]

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.set
documentation:
Return a new QueryParams instance, setting the value of a key.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123")
q = q.set("a", "456")
assert q == httpx.QueryParams("a=456")

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.add
documentation:
Return a new QueryParams instance, setting or appending the value of a key.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123")
q = q.add("a", "456")
assert q == httpx.QueryParams("a=123&a=456")

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.remove
documentation:
Return a new QueryParams instance, removing the value of a key.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123")
q = q.remove("a")
assert q == httpx.QueryParams("")

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.merge
documentation:
Return a new QueryParams instance, updated with.

Usage:

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123")
q = q.merge({"b": "456"})
assert q == httpx.QueryParams("a=123&b=456")

q = httpx.QueryParams("a=123")
q = q.merge({"a": "456", "b": "789"})
assert q == httpx.QueryParams("a=456&b=789")

methods.name: httpx._urls.QueryParams.__str__
documentation:
Note that we use '%20' encoding for spaces, and treat '/' as a safe
character.

See https://github.com/encode/httpx/issues/2536 and
https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.parse.html#urllib.parse.urlencode
URL query parameters, as a multi-dict.

functions.name: httpx._api.request
documentation:
Sends an HTTP request.

**Parameters:**

- **method** - HTTP method for the new `Request` object: `GET`, `OPTIONS`,

`HEAD`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, or `DELETE`.
\* **url** - URL for the new `Request` object.
\* **params** - *(optional)* Query parameters to include in the URL, as a
string, dictionary, or sequence of two-tuples.
\* **content** - *(optional)* Binary content to include in the body of the
request, as bytes or a byte iterator.
\* **data** - *(optional)* Form data to include in the body of the request,
as a dictionary.
\* **files** - *(optional)* A dictionary of upload files to include in the
body of the request.
\* **json** - *(optional)* A JSON serializable object to include in the body
of the request.
\* **headers** - *(optional)* Dictionary of HTTP headers to include in the
request.
\* **cookies** - *(optional)* Dictionary of Cookie items to include in the
request.
\* **auth** - *(optional)* An authentication class to use when sending the
request.
\* **proxy** - *(optional)* A proxy URL where all the traffic should be routed.
\* **proxies** - *(optional)* A dictionary mapping proxy keys to proxy URLs.
\* **timeout** - *(optional)* The timeout configuration to use when sending
the request.
\* **follow_redirects** - *(optional)* Enables or disables HTTP redirects.
\* **verify** - *(optional)* SSL certificates (a.k.a CA bundle) used to
verify the identity of requested hosts. Either `True` (default CA bundle),
a path to an SSL certificate file, an `ssl.SSLContext`, or `False`
(which will disable verification).
\* **cert** - *(optional)* An SSL certificate used by the requested host
to authenticate the client. Either a path to an SSL certificate file, or
two-tuple of (certificate file, key file), or a three-tuple of (certificate
file, key file, password).
\* **trust_env** - *(optional)* Enables or disables usage of environment
variables for configuration.

**Returns:** `Response`

Usage:

`` `
\>>> import httpx
\>>> response = httpx.request('GET', 'https://httpbin.org/get')
\>>> response
<Response [200 OK]>
` ``

functions.name: httpx._api.stream
documentation:
Alternative to `httpx.request()` that streams the response body
instead of loading it into memory at once.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

See also: [Streaming Responses][0]

[0]: /quickstart#streaming-responses

functions.name: httpx._api.get
documentation:
Sends a `GET` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

Note that the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available
on this function, as `GET` requests should not include a request body.

functions.name: httpx._api.options
documentation:
Sends an `OPTIONS` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

Note that the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available
on this function, as `OPTIONS` requests should not include a request body.

functions.name: httpx._api.head
documentation:
Sends a `HEAD` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

Note that the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available
on this function, as `HEAD` requests should not include a request body.

functions.name: httpx._api.post
documentation:
Sends a `POST` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

functions.name: httpx._api.put
documentation:
Sends a `PUT` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

functions.name: httpx._api.patch
documentation:
Sends a `PATCH` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

functions.name: httpx._api.delete
documentation:
Sends a `DELETE` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

Note that the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available
on this function, as `DELETE` requests should not include a request body.

functions.name: httpx._utils.normalize_header_key
documentation: Coerce str/bytes into a strictly byte-wise HTTP header key.

functions.name: httpx._utils.normalize_header_value
documentation: Coerce str/bytes into a strictly byte-wise HTTP header value.

functions.name: httpx._utils.primitive_value_to_str
documentation:
Coerce a primitive data type into a string value.

Note that we prefer JSON-style 'true'/'false' for boolean values here.

functions.name: httpx._utils.is_known_encoding
documentation: Return `True` if `encoding` is a known codec.

functions.name: httpx._utils.format_form_param
documentation: Encode a name/value pair within a multipart form.

functions.name: httpx._utils.parse_header_links
documentation:
Returns a list of parsed link headers, for more info see:
<https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Link>
The generic syntax of those is:
Link: \< uri-reference >; param1=value1; param2="value2"
So for instance:
Link; '\<<http:/.../front.jpeg>>; type="image/jpeg",\<<http://.../back.jpeg>>;'
would return

> \[
>
> : {"url": "<http:/.../front.jpeg>", "type": "image/jpeg"},
>   {"url": "<http://.../back.jpeg>"},
>
> \]

```
param value

HTTP Link entity-header field

return

list of parsed link headers
```

functions.name: httpx._utils.same_origin
documentation: Return 'True' if the given URLs share the same origin.

functions.name: httpx._utils.is_https_redirect
documentation: Return 'True' if 'location' is a HTTPS upgrade of 'url'

functions.name: httpx._utils.get_environment_proxies
documentation: Gets proxy information from the environment

functions.name: httpx._utils.peek_filelike_length
documentation:
Given a file-like stream object, return its length in number of bytes
without reading it into memory.

classes.name: httpx._utils.URLPattern

methods.name: httpx._utils.URLPattern.priority
documentation:
The priority allows URLPattern instances to be sortable, so that
we can match from most specific to least specific.

A utility class currently used for making lookups against proxy keys...

\# Wildcard matching...
\>>> pattern = URLPattern("all://")
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("http://example.com"))
True

\# Witch scheme matching...
\>>> pattern = URLPattern("<https://>")
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<https://example.com>"))
True
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<http://example.com>"))
False

\# With domain matching...
\>>> pattern = URLPattern("<https://example.com>")
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<https://example.com>"))
True
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<http://example.com>"))
False
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<https://other.com>"))
False

\# Wildcard scheme, with domain matching...
\>>> pattern = URLPattern("all://example.com")
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("https://example.com"))
True
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("http://example.com"))
True
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("https://other.com"))
False

\# With port matching...
\>>> pattern = URLPattern("<https://example.com:1234>")
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<https://example.com:1234>"))
True
\>>> pattern.matches(httpx.URL("<https://example.com>"))
False

functions.name: httpx._main.main
documentation:
An HTTP command line client.
Sends a request and displays the response.

classes.name: httpx._models.Headers

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.encoding
documentation:
Header encoding is mandated as ascii, but we allow fallbacks to utf-8
or iso-8859-1.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.raw
documentation: Returns a list of the raw header items, as byte pairs.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.items
documentation:
Return `(key, value)` items of headers. Concatenate headers
into a single comma separated value when a key occurs multiple times.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.multi_items
documentation:
Return a list of `(key, value)` pairs of headers. Allow multiple
occurrences of the same key without concatenating into a single
comma separated value.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.get
documentation:
Return a header value. If multiple occurrences of the header occur
then concatenate them together with commas.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.get_list
documentation:
Return a list of all header values for a given key.
If `split_commas=True` is passed, then any comma separated header
values are split into multiple return strings.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.__getitem__
documentation:
Return a single header value.

If there are multiple headers with the same key, then we concatenate
them with commas. See: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-3.2.2

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.__setitem__
documentation:
Set the header `key` to `value`, removing any duplicate entries.
Retains insertion order.

methods.name: httpx._models.Headers.__delitem__
documentation: Remove the header `key`.
HTTP headers, as a case-insensitive multi-dict.

methods.name: httpx._models.Request.read
documentation: Read and return the request content.

methods.name: httpx._models.Request.aread
documentation: Read and return the request content.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.elapsed
documentation:
Returns the time taken for the complete request/response
cycle to complete.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.request
documentation: Returns the request instance associated to the current response.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.url
documentation: Returns the URL for which the request was made.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.encoding
documentation:
Return an encoding to use for decoding the byte content into text.
The priority for determining this is given by...

* `.encoding = <>` has been set explicitly.
* The encoding as specified by the charset parameter in the Content-Type header.
* The encoding as determined by `default_encoding`, which may either be
  a string like "utf-8" indicating the encoding to use, or may be a callable
  which enables charset autodetection.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.encoding
documentation:
Set the encoding to use for decoding the byte content into text.

If the `text` attribute has been accessed, attempting to set the
encoding will throw a ValueError.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.charset_encoding
documentation: Return the encoding, as specified by the Content-Type header.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response._get_content_decoder
documentation:
Returns a decoder instance which can be used to decode the raw byte
content, depending on the Content-Encoding used in the response.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.is_informational
documentation: A property which is `True` for 1xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.is_success
documentation: A property which is `True` for 2xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.is_redirect
documentation:
A property which is `True` for 3xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

Note that not all responses with a 3xx status code indicate a URL redirect.

Use `response.has_redirect_location` to determine responses with a properly
formed URL redirection.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.is_client_error
documentation: A property which is `True` for 4xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.is_server_error
documentation: A property which is `True` for 5xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.is_error
documentation: A property which is `True` for 4xx and 5xx status codes, `False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.has_redirect_location
documentation:
Returns True for 3xx responses with a properly formed URL redirection,
`False` otherwise.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.raise_for_status
documentation: Raise the `HTTPStatusError` if one occurred.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.links
documentation: Returns the parsed header links of the response, if any

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.read
documentation: Read and return the response content.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.iter_bytes
documentation:
A byte-iterator over the decoded response content.
This allows us to handle gzip, deflate, brotli, and zstd encoded responses.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.iter_text
documentation:
A str-iterator over the decoded response content
that handles both gzip, deflate, etc but also detects the content's
string encoding.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.iter_raw
documentation: A byte-iterator over the raw response content.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.close
documentation:
Close the response and release the connection.
Automatically called if the response body is read to completion.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.aread
documentation: Read and return the response content.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.aiter_bytes
documentation:
A byte-iterator over the decoded response content.
This allows us to handle gzip, deflate, brotli, and zstd encoded responses.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.aiter_text
documentation:
A str-iterator over the decoded response content
that handles both gzip, deflate, etc but also detects the content's
string encoding.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.aiter_raw
documentation: A byte-iterator over the raw response content.

methods.name: httpx._models.Response.aclose
documentation:
Close the response and release the connection.
Automatically called if the response body is read to completion.

classes.name: httpx._models.Cookies

methods.name: httpx._models.Cookies.extract_cookies
documentation: Loads any cookies based on the response `Set-Cookie` headers.

methods.name: httpx._models.Cookies.set_cookie_header
documentation: Sets an appropriate 'Cookie:' HTTP header on the `Request`.

methods.name: httpx._models.Cookies.set
documentation: Set a cookie value by name. May optionally include domain and path.

methods.name: httpx._models.Cookies.get
documentation:
Get a cookie by name. May optionally include domain and path
in order to specify exactly which cookie to retrieve.

methods.name: httpx._models.Cookies.delete
documentation:
Delete a cookie by name. May optionally include domain and path
in order to specify exactly which cookie to delete.

methods.name: httpx._models.Cookies.clear
documentation:
Delete all cookies. Optionally include a domain and path in
order to only delete a subset of all the cookies.

inner_classes.name: httpx._models.Cookies._CookieCompatRequest
documentation:
Wraps a `Request` instance up in a compatibility interface suitable
for use with `CookieJar` operations.

inner_classes.name: httpx._models.Cookies._CookieCompatResponse
documentation:
Wraps a `Request` instance up in a compatibility interface suitable
for use with `CookieJar` operations.
HTTP Cookies, as a mutable mapping.

classes.name: httpx._client.UseClientDefault
documentation:
For some parameters such as `auth=...` and `timeout=...` we need to be able
to indicate the default "unset" state, in a way that is distinctly different
to using `None`.

The default "unset" state indicates that whatever default is set on the
client should be used. This is different to setting `None`, which
explicitly disables the parameter, possibly overriding a client default.

For example we use `timeout=USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT` in the `request()` signature.
Omitting the `timeout` parameter will send a request using whatever default
timeout has been configured on the client. Including `timeout=None` will
ensure no timeout is used.

Note that user code shouldn't need to use the `USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT` constant,
but it is used internally when a parameter is not included.

classes.name: httpx._client.BoundSyncStream
documentation:
A byte stream that is bound to a given response instance, and that
ensures the `response.elapsed` is set once the response is closed.

classes.name: httpx._client.BoundAsyncStream
documentation:
An async byte stream that is bound to a given response instance, and that
ensures the `response.elapsed` is set once the response is closed.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.is_closed
documentation: Check if the client being closed

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.auth
documentation:
Authentication class used when none is passed at the request-level.

See also [Authentication][0].

[0]: /quickstart/#authentication

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.base_url
documentation: Base URL to use when sending requests with relative URLs.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.headers
documentation: HTTP headers to include when sending requests.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.cookies
documentation: Cookie values to include when sending requests.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.params
documentation: Query parameters to include in the URL when sending requests.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient.build_request
documentation:
Build and return a request instance.

* The `params`, `headers` and `cookies` arguments
are merged with any values set on the client.
* The `url` argument is merged with any `base_url` set on the client.

See also: [Request instances][0]

[0]: /advanced/clients/#request-instances

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._merge_url
documentation:
Merge a URL argument together with any 'base_url' on the client,
to create the URL used for the outgoing request.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._merge_cookies
documentation:
Merge a cookies argument together with any cookies on the client,
to create the cookies used for the outgoing request.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._merge_headers
documentation:
Merge a headers argument together with any headers on the client,
to create the headers used for the outgoing request.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._merge_queryparams
documentation:
Merge a queryparams argument together with any queryparams on the client,
to create the queryparams used for the outgoing request.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._build_redirect_request
documentation:
Given a request and a redirect response, return a new request that
should be used to effect the redirect.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._redirect_method
documentation:
When being redirected we may want to change the method of the request
based on certain specs or browser behavior.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._redirect_url
documentation: Return the URL for the redirect to follow.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._redirect_headers
documentation: Return the headers that should be used for the redirect request.

methods.name: httpx._client.BaseClient._redirect_stream
documentation: Return the body that should be used for the redirect request.

classes.name: httpx._client.Client

methods.name: httpx._client.Client._transport_for_url
documentation:
Returns the transport instance that should be used for a given URL.
This will either be the standard connection pool, or a proxy.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.request
documentation:
Build and send a request.

Equivalent to:

```python
request = client.build_request(...)
response = client.send(request, ...)
```

See `Client.build_request()`, `Client.send()` and
[Merging of configuration][0] for how the various parameters
are merged with client-level configuration.

[0]: /advanced/clients/#merging-of-configuration

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.stream
documentation:
Alternative to `httpx.request()` that streams the response body
instead of loading it into memory at once.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

See also: [Streaming Responses][0]

[0]: /quickstart#streaming-responses

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.send
documentation:
Send a request.

The request is sent as-is, unmodified.

Typically you'll want to build one with `Client.build_request()`
so that any client-level configuration is merged into the request,
but passing an explicit `httpx.Request()` is supported as well.

See also: [Request instances][0]

[0]: /advanced/clients/#request-instances

methods.name: httpx._client.Client._send_single_request
documentation: Sends a single request, without handling any redirections.

documentation:
Send a `GET` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.options
documentation:
Send an `OPTIONS` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.head
documentation:
Send a `HEAD` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.post
documentation:
Send a `POST` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.put
documentation:
Send a `PUT` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.patch
documentation:

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.delete
documentation:
Send a `DELETE` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.Client.close
documentation: Close transport and proxies.

An HTTP client, with connection pooling, HTTP/2, redirects, cookie persistence, etc.

It can be shared between threads.

Usage:

`` `python
\>>> client = httpx.Client()
\>>> response = client.get('https://example.org')
` ``

**Parameters:**

- **auth** - *(optional)* An authentication class to use when sending

requests.
\* **params** - *(optional)* Query parameters to include in request URLs, as
a string, dictionary, or sequence of two-tuples.
\* **headers** - *(optional)* Dictionary of HTTP headers to include when
sending requests.
\* **cookies** - *(optional)* Dictionary of Cookie items to include when
sending requests.
\* **verify** - *(optional)* SSL certificates (a.k.a CA bundle) used to
verify the identity of requested hosts. Either `True` (default CA bundle),
a path to an SSL certificate file, an `ssl.SSLContext`, or `False`
(which will disable verification).
\* **cert** - *(optional)* An SSL certificate used by the requested host
to authenticate the client. Either a path to an SSL certificate file, or
two-tuple of (certificate file, key file), or a three-tuple of (certificate
file, key file, password).
\* **http2** - *(optional)* A boolean indicating if HTTP/2 support should be
enabled. Defaults to `False`.
\* **proxy** - *(optional)* A proxy URL where all the traffic should be routed.
\* **proxies** - *(optional)* A dictionary mapping proxy keys to proxy
URLs.
\* **timeout** - *(optional)* The timeout configuration to use when sending
requests.
\* **limits** - *(optional)* The limits configuration to use.
\* **max_redirects** - *(optional)* The maximum number of redirect responses
that should be followed.
\* **base_url** - *(optional)* A URL to use as the base when building
request URLs.
\* **transport** - *(optional)* A transport class to use for sending requests
over the network.
\* **app** - *(optional)* An WSGI application to send requests to,
rather than sending actual network requests.
\* **trust_env** - *(optional)* Enables or disables usage of environment
variables for configuration.
\* **default_encoding** - *(optional)* The default encoding to use for decoding
response text, if no charset information is included in a response Content-Type
header. Set to a callable for automatic character set detection. Default: "utf-8".

classes.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient._transport_for_url
documentation:
Returns the transport instance that should be used for a given URL.
This will either be the standard connection pool, or a proxy.

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.request
documentation:
Build and send a request.

Equivalent to:

```python
request = client.build_request(...)
response = await client.send(request, ...)
```

See `AsyncClient.build_request()`, `AsyncClient.send()`
and [Merging of configuration][0] for how the various parameters
are merged with client-level configuration.

[0]: /advanced/clients/#merging-of-configuration

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.stream
documentation:
Alternative to `httpx.request()` that streams the response body
instead of loading it into memory at once.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

See also: [Streaming Responses][0]

[0]: /quickstart#streaming-responses

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.send
documentation:
Send a request.

The request is sent as-is, unmodified.

Typically you'll want to build one with `AsyncClient.build_request()`
so that any client-level configuration is merged into the request,
but passing an explicit `httpx.Request()` is supported as well.

See also: [Request instances][0]

[0]: /advanced/clients/#request-instances

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient._send_single_request
documentation: Sends a single request, without handling any redirections.

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.get
documentation:
Send a `GET` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.options

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.head

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.post

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.put

Send a `PUT` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.patch
documentation:
Send a `PATCH` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.delete

methods.name: httpx._client.AsyncClient.aclose
documentation: Close transport and proxies.

An asynchronous HTTP client, with connection pooling, HTTP/2, redirects,
cookie persistence, etc.

It can be shared between tasks.

Usage:

`` `python
\>>> async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
\>>>     response = await client.get('https://example.org')
` ``

**Parameters:**

- **auth** - *(optional)* An authentication class to use when sending

requests.
\* **params** - *(optional)* Query parameters to include in request URLs, as
a string, dictionary, or sequence of two-tuples.
\* **headers** - *(optional)* Dictionary of HTTP headers to include when
sending requests.
\* **cookies** - *(optional)* Dictionary of Cookie items to include when
sending requests.
\* **verify** - *(optional)* SSL certificates (a.k.a CA bundle) used to
verify the identity of requested hosts. Either `True` (default CA bundle),
a path to an SSL certificate file, an `ssl.SSLContext`, or `False`
(which will disable verification).
\* **cert** - *(optional)* An SSL certificate used by the requested host
to authenticate the client. Either a path to an SSL certificate file, or
two-tuple of (certificate file, key file), or a three-tuple of (certificate
file, key file, password).
\* **http2** - *(optional)* A boolean indicating if HTTP/2 support should be
enabled. Defaults to `False`.
\* **proxy** - *(optional)* A proxy URL where all the traffic should be routed.
\* **proxies** - *(optional)* A dictionary mapping HTTP protocols to proxy
URLs.
\* **timeout** - *(optional)* The timeout configuration to use when sending
requests.
\* **limits** - *(optional)* The limits configuration to use.
\* **max_redirects** - *(optional)* The maximum number of redirect responses
that should be followed.
\* **base_url** - *(optional)* A URL to use as the base when building
request URLs.
\* **transport** - *(optional)* A transport class to use for sending requests
over the network.
\* **app** - *(optional)* An ASGI application to send requests to,
rather than sending actual network requests.
\* **trust_env** - *(optional)* Enables or disables usage of environment
variables for configuration.
\* **default_encoding** - *(optional)* The default encoding to use for decoding
response text, if no charset information is included in a response Content-Type
header. Set to a callable for automatic character set detection. Default: "utf-8".
